sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Training computer-use agents (CUAs) -- models that interact with graphical desktops through screenshots and keyboard/mouse actions -- requires large-scale, diverse trajectory data collected in full desktop environments. The largest public resource, AgentNet (22.5K human trajectories), leads to negative transfer when used for supervised fine-tuning (SFT): continuing training UI-TARS 7B on AgentNet causes OSWorld success rate to fall from 26.3% to 8-10%. We present ProCUA-SFT, a dataset of 3.1M step-level SFT samples distilled from 93K synthetic trajectories across 2,484 application combinations. The dataset is produced by a fully automated pipeline that (i) synthesizes grounded tasks on live desktops seeded with real-world content -- 912 spreadsheets from SpreadsheetBench, approximately 10K permissively-licensed presentations from Zenodo10K, and multi-application OSWorld configs -- and (ii) verifies each task's feasibility through binary precondition checking before rollout. A single VLM (Kimi-K2.5) serves as goal generator, precondition judge, and trajectory executor, eliminating planner-actor capability gaps. Each trajectory is expanded into step-prefix samples that exactly reproduce the context layout seen at inference time. Fine-tuning UI-TARS 7B on ProCUA-SFT for one epoch yields 45.0% on OSWorld -- an 18.7 percentage-point improvement over the base model and over 35% above AgentNet-trained counterparts. A subset of ProCUA was incorporated into the training data for the Nemotron 3 Nano Omni model, contributing to its computer-use capabilities.

sections.title: Start
sections.content: 6 2 0 2 5 1 ] . [ 1 1 2 3 7 1 . 6 0 6 2 : r 2026-6 ProCUA-SFT Technical Report Jaehun Jung*,1,2, Ximing Lu*,1,2, Brandon Cui*,1, Muhammad Khalifa1, Shaokun Zhang1, Hao Zhang1, Jin Xu1, Amala Sanjay Deshmukh1, Karan Sapra1, Andrew Tao1, Yejin Choi1, Jan Kautz1, Mingjie Liu*,1,, Yi Dong1, Training computer-use agents (CUAs)models that interact with graphical desktops through screenshots and keyboard/mouse actionsrequires large-scale, diverse trajectory data collected in full desktop environments. The largest public resource, AgentNet (22.5K human trajectories), leads to negative transfer when used for supervised fine-tuning (SFT): continuing training UI-TARS 7B on AgentNet causes OSWorld success rate to fall from 26.3% to 810%. We present ProCUA-SFT, dataset of 3.1M step-level SFT samples distilled from 93K synthetic trajectories across 2,484 application combinations. The dataset is produced by fully automated pipeline that (i) synthesizes grounded tasks on live desktops seeded with real-world content912 spreadsheets from SpreadsheetBench, 10K permissively-licensed presentations from Zenodo10K, and multi-application OSWorld configsand (ii) verifies each tasks feasibility through binary precondition checking before rollout. single VLM (Kimi-K2.5) serves as goal generator, precondition judge, and trajectory executor, eliminating planneractor capability gaps. Each trajectory is expanded into step-prefix samples that exactly reproduce the context layout seen at inference time. Fine-tuning UI-TARS 7B on ProCUA-SFT for one epoch yields 45.0% on OSWorldan 18.7 percentage-point improvement over the base model and over 35% above AgentNet-trained counterparts. subset of ProCUA was incorporated into the training data for the Nemotron 3 Nano Omni model (Deshmukh et al., 2026), contributing to its computer-use capabilities. Data on Hugging Face: ProCUA-SFT 1. Introduction Computer-use agents (CUAs) perceive graphical desktops through screenshots and act through keyboard and mouse commands. Yet progress remains early: open-source CUAs such as UI-TARS-7B (Qin et al., 2025) and OpenCUA-7B (Wang et al., 2025b) have reached only 26.3% and 25.0% on OSWorld (Xie et al., 2024b), benchmark of realistic desktop tasks, and further progress is bottlenecked by training data. Unlike text-only instruction tuning, each CUA trajectory requires fully instantiated desktopbooted VM, installed applications, realistic file contentmaking large-scale collection substantially more expensive than curating text corpora. The largest public CUA training resource, AgentNet (Wang et al., 2025b), provides 22.5K human-annotated trajectories spanning three operating systems. Despite its scale and scope, fine-tuning UI-TARS 7B on AgentNet for one epoch causes OSWorld success rate to fall from 26.3% to 810% (Figure 2). We attribute this negative transfer to three factors: limited task diversity (the majority of trajectories are single-application workflows with median of 17 steps), the absence of complex cross-application reasoning tasks, and annotation noise inherent in crowd-sourced demonstrations. These observations motivate fully synthetic approachbut naïve synthetic generation faces its own challenge: LLM-generated tasks often reference non-existent files or unavailable applications, wasting rollout compute on infeasible goals. We introduce ProCUA-SFT, dataset of 3.1M step-level SFT samples from 93K synthetic trajectories spanning 2,484 application combinations. The pipeline is built around single VLMKimi-K2.5 (Kimi Team et al., 2026b)that serves as goal generator, precondition judge, and trajectory executor. Four design decisions define the pipeline: 1. Grounded task synthesis with precondition verification. The VLM generates each task goal together with set of binary preconditions (e.g., Does the file Q3.xlsx exist on the Desktop?). separate judge prompt independently verifies each precondition against the current desktop screenshot and OS-level 1NVIDIA 2University of Washington *Equal contribution Corresponding authors 2026 NVIDIA. All rights reserved. ProCUA-SFT Technical Report config; only goals whose preconditions all pass proceed to rollout, with failed verdicts fed back for iterative retry (3.1). 2. Real-world content seeding. Desktops are initialized with 912 real-world spreadsheets from SpreadsheetBench (Ma et al., 2024a) (tables exceeding 100 columns and 20K rows), 10K permissively-licensed presentations from Zenodo10K (Forceless, 2024a), and multi-application configs from OSWorld (Xie et al., 2024b). This yields tasks that operate on genuine complex documentsformulas, charts, multi-sheet referencesrather than empty or default application states (3.2). 3. Single-VLM rollout. Using the same model for goal synthesis and trajectory execution closes the planner actor capability gap: the model never proposes goals beyond what it can carry out, which maximizes the fraction of trajectories that reach successful completion (3.3). 4. Step-prefix expansion. Each trajectory of 𝑇 steps yields 𝑇 training samples; sample 𝑡 contains the full screenshotaction history up to step 𝑡, reproducing the growing-context layout the model encounters at inference time (3.4). The pipeline runs on decoupled infrastructurelocal KVM via Singularity and serverless NVCFenabling parallel collection across heterogeneous compute (3.5). After one epoch of SFT on UI-TARS 7B, ProCUA-SFT achieves 45.0% on OSWorld, an 18.7 pp gain over the base model and more than 35 pp above AgentNet-trained models (Figure 2). As evidence of its utility, portion of ProCUA was incorporated into the training data for the Nemotron 3 Nano Omni model (Deshmukh et al., 2026), contributing to that models computer-use capabilities. 2. Related Work Vision-based GUI agents. Early GUI agents relied on structured inputsDOM trees, accessibility APIsto perceive and act on interfaces (Shi et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2018; Deng et al., 2023). Screenshot-based models remove this dependency. Pix2Act (Shaw et al., 2023) trains Pix2Struct encoderdecoder with demonstrations and tree-search self-play on MiniWoB++ and web tasks. CogAgent (Hong et al., 2023) introduces dual-resolution visual encoding (11201120) for recognizing small UI elements. SeeClick (Cheng et al., 2024) and Ferret-UI (You et al., 2024) advance GUI groundingthrough grounding pre-training and resolution-adaptive encoding for mobile UIs, respectivelywhile OS-Atlas (Wu et al., 2025) and UGround (Gou et al., 2025) scale grounding corpora to 13M+ and 10M elements across platforms. At the full-system level, UI-TARS (Qin et al., 2025) combines enhanced perception with system-2 reasoning to reach state-of-the-art on 10+ benchmarks; ShowUI (Lin et al., 2025) reduces visual token cost via UI-guided selection; Aguvis (Xu et al., 2025b) trains pure-vision agent across web, mobile, and desktop; and ScribeAgent (Shen et al., 2024) converts screenshots to structured text, bypassing vision entirely. ProCUA-SFT is training-data contribution complementary to all of these architectures. Benchmarks. OSWorld (Xie et al., 2024b) provides 369 tasks in real Linux/Windows/macOS VMs with unrestricted keyboard/mouse control and is our primary evaluation target. WebArena (Zhou et al., 2024) (812 tasks) and VisualWebArena (Koh et al., 2024) (910 tasks) evaluate web agents on self-hosted sites. Windows Agent Arena (Bonatti et al., 2024) provides 150+ Windows desktop tasks; AndroidWorld (Rawles et al., 2024) offers 116 programmatic tasks across 20 Android apps; and MiniWoB++ (Liu et al., 2018) provides 100+ simplified web micro-tasks. Training data for GUI agents. Human-demonstration datasets include AITW (Rawles et al., 2023) (715K Android episodes), Mind2Web (Deng et al., 2023) (2K web tasks with crowdsourced actions), and AgentNet (Wang et al., 2025b) (22.5K cross-platform trajectories). AgentNet is the largest desktop-targeted resource to date, yet as we show in 4, it causes negative transfer when used for SFT. Recent works synthesize trajectories automatically to bypass the cost of human annotation. In web environments, AgentTrek (Xu et al., 2025a) replays web tutorials to produce 10K trajectories, and Explorer (Pahuja et al., 2025) generates 94K trajectories via exploration across 49K URLs. InSTA (Trabucco et al., 2025) annotates 150K websites and filters by LLM-judged success; Go-Browse (Gandhi and Neubig, 2025) applies Go-Explorestyle graph search; and SynthAgent (Wang et al., 2025c) introduces dual refinement of tasks and trajectories. In desktop settings, AgentSynth (Xie et al., 2025) composes subtasks into long-horizon tasks via ProCUA-SFT Technical Report information asymmetry, while PC Agent (He et al., 2024) and PC Agent-E (He et al., 2025) augment small human-trajectory seed sets with LLM-synthesized alternatives. Orthogonally, DigiRL (Bai et al., 2024) applies online RL to Android device control, and AgentRefine (Fu et al., 2025) refines failed trajectories through self-correction before SFT. ProCUA-SFT differs from prior synthesis work in three respects: (i) it verifies task feasibility through in-loop binary precondition checking rather than post-hoc filtering; (ii) it seeds desktops with externally sourced complex content (real spreadsheets, real presentations) rather than default or empty states; and (iii) at 3.1M samples from 93K trajectories across 2,484 application combinations, it is the largest open-source desktop CUA training dataset. 3. ProCUA-SFT The goal of ProCUA-SFT is to provide diverse, grounded, and difficult computer-use trajectories at scale large enough to drive supervised fine-tuning. Three design choices distinguish the pipeline from naive run strong agent on benchmark and harvest its rollouts recipe: (i) tasks are synthesized from the desktop, with explicit precondition checks, so that the goal is provably grounded in what the agent can actually do (3.1); (ii) the desktop is seeded with real, externally sourced contentreal-world spreadsheets and presentations under permissive licensesso that hard cross-application reasoning is possible at all (3.2); and (iii) the data-collection infrastructure decouples model inference from environment execution and is portable across heterogeneous compute backends, which is what lets the same code scale data collection from either local KVM hosts or serverless cloud VMs (3.5). Throughout the pipeline we use single VLM, Kimi-K2.5 (Kimi Team et al., 2026a), for all policy rolesgoal generation, requirement verification, and trajectory rolloutunder different prompts. Figure 1 summarizes the resulting end-to-end flow. 3.1. Grounded Task Synthesis common failure mode of synthetic CUA data is the infeasible task: the generator asks the agent to open the Q3 report on the Desktop when no such file exists, or to send the email draft when no mail client is configured. Such tasks waste rollout budget andworseteach the SFT model to invent state. Conversely, restricting tasks to whatever is visible on the screen is too conservative: it misses files placed by the setup config, browser tabs not yet focused, and applications launchable from the system menu but not currently in the foreground. We address this by treating task synthesis as grounded generation problem with an in-loop verifier. Let 𝒮 denote the space of desktop states, and let 𝜋𝐺 and 𝜋𝐽 denote goal-generator policy and Judge policy; in our implementation both are realized by the same VLM with different prompts. Generating goals with verifiable requirements. Rather than asking the generator for goal in isolation, we ask it to emit goal together with small set of binary preconditions that must hold in the current desktop for the goal to be achievable: 𝜋𝐺 : (𝑠0, 𝑐, 𝑎, 𝑉<𝑘) (𝑔(𝑘), 𝑅(𝑘)), 𝑅(𝑘) = {𝑟1, 𝑟2, . . . , 𝑟5}, (1) where 𝑠0 𝒮 is the initial screenshot; 𝑐 is the structured OSWorld setup config (files placed on disk, applications launched, browser tabs opened, shell commands run); 𝑎 is style-anchor instruction sampled from AgentNet (Wang et al., 2025a); 𝑉<𝑘 is the set of requirementverdict pairs accumulated from prior failed attempts on the same VM (with 𝑉<1 = ); and each requirement 𝑟𝑖 : 𝒮 {0, 1} is an objective, binary predicate on desktop state. For example, alongside goal of open the quarterly report and add chart of the revenue column, the generator might emit requirements such as Does Q3.xlsx exist on the Desktop?, Is LibreOffice Calc installed?, and Is the screen unlocked and at the desktop?. These precondition questions are precisely what the Judge will subsequently adjudicate. Verifying with the Judge and verdict-conditioned retry. Each requirement is independently evaluated by the Judge policy against the current observation, producing yes/no verdict 𝑣(𝑘) 𝑖 = 𝜋𝐽 (𝑟𝑖, 𝑠0, 𝑐) {0, 1}. (2) ProCUA-SFT Technical Report Figure 1 ProCUA-SFT pipeline. fresh desktop is seeded from one of three task-config sources (OSWorld, SpreadsheetBench, Zenodo10K). The same VLM then synthesizes grounded goal from the screenshot and OS state, rolls out trajectory toward it, and signals success/failure via reserved terminate call. Each trajectory is expanded into multiple step-prefix SFT samples in ShareGPT format. 4 ProCUA-SFT Technical Report We accept the candidate goal iff )}𝑖 and re-invoke 𝜋𝐺, up to maximum of 𝐾 rounds. The verdict feedback carried in 𝑉<𝑘 is what steers subsequent attempts away from preconditions already proven false on this VM and toward conditions the environment can actually satisfy. = 1. Otherwise we update 𝑉<𝑘+1 = 𝑉<𝑘 {(𝑟𝑖, 𝑣(𝑘) 𝑖 𝑣(𝑘) 𝑖 𝑖 Two properties make this loop grounding mechanism rather than self-discipline scaffolding. First, requiring 𝜋𝐺 to commit to binary, verifiable 𝑟𝑖 alongside the goal yields hand-off that 𝜋𝐽 can actually adjudicatefluent but ungrounded preconditions are exactly what the verifier rejects. Second, because 𝑐 is supplied to both 𝜋𝐺 and 𝜋𝐽 , the loop closes over resources promised by the setup but not visible at 𝑡=0 (a file deposited on disk by an upload_file step, shell-launched server that has not yet rendered window), broadening task diversity beyond surface visual content. We run the verify-and-retry loop (𝐾 > 1) on portion of the released data and single-shot variant (𝐾 = 1, no Judge call) on the remainder; both share the formulation in Eq. (1). 3.2. Sourcing Complex, Permissively-Licensed Content The hardest CUA tasksreading number from spreadsheet, looking it up on the web, summarizing the result into slide deckare bottlenecked not by the agents action space but by the content on the desktop. Empty Calc workbooks and stock template presentations do not support such workflows. We therefore seed each trajectory from one of three task-config sources, two of which inject genuinely complex external content: Vanilla OSWorld snapshots. OSWorld (Xie et al., 2024a) ships set of example setup configs that, after we exclude entries depending on Google Drive credentials, give us multi-application starting states covering web browsing, terminal sessions, and the LibreOffice suite. We use these for breadth. SpreadsheetBench. SpreadsheetBench (Ma et al., 2024b) is collection of 912 real-world spreadsheets harvested from online Excel forums, with tables that exceed 100 columns and 20,000 rows and that contain non-standard layouts, multiple sheets, and rich non-textual content. We adapt only the workbooks (not the benchmarks reference solutions or test cases) into setup configs that upload the .xlsx to the Desktop and open it in LibreOffice Calc. The benchmark releases the workbooks under research license; we use them only to instantiate desktop state and do not redistribute the files themselves. Zenodo10K presentations. Zenodo10K (Forceless, 2024b) is public collection of 10,000 .pptx files mined from the Zenodo open-research repository. We use the subset released under permissive reuse license (CC-BY 4.0). Beyond its raw scale, the dataset preserves Zenodos parent-record IDs, which group co-published artifactsa property we exploit to seed multi-file desktop states (described below). Seeding multi-file desktops via Zenodo grouping. Real users rarely keep single isolated document on their desktop: drafts, supplements, and related decks tend to sit alongside each other, and class of useful tasks (compare two versions, fold an appendix into main deck, locate file by name in the file manager) only makes sense in that multi-file setting. Sampling presentations independently would underspecify this startup state. We therefore sample one Zenodo parent record per trajectory, upload all of its member files to the Desktop, and open between one and three of them in LibreOffice Impress (with weights 0.5/0.3/0.2); the unopened files remain as bystander documents that the agent can discover via the file manager. The combination of grounded goal synthesis (3.1) and these external seed corpora is what makes the resulting tasks simultaneously hard and feasible: the generator is asked to invent goal that exercises the spreadsheet or presentation that has been opened in front of it, but is constrained by binary requirements to only ask for things the desktop can actually deliver. Per-source goal-synthesis prompts further specialize this objectivee.g., the SpreadsheetBench prompt requires the goal to reference visible columns, sheet names, and data patterns and to chain 23 sub-tasks (cross-sheet aggregation, charting, export to Writer/CSV), while multi-app variant of the Zenodo prompt encourages workflows that span Impress, Calc, Writer, Chrome, the file manager, and the terminal. 3.3. Single-Model Rollouts and Self-Termination Once goal is synthesized, the same VLM rolls out trajectory toward it. Using single model for both goal synthesis and acting is deliberate: the generators notion of what is achievable coincides with the actors actual capability, which prunes the gap in which stronger planner proposes goals that weaker actor cannot 5 ProCUA-SFT Technical Report execute. At each step the model receives the current screenshot, the goal, and windowed history (see below), and produces chain of thought followed by an Action/Code pair. The code is either raw pyautogui (click, moveTo, hotkey, typewrite, scroll, dragTo, . . . ) over absolute screen coordinates, or one of two reserved control functions: wait() for installs and slow UI loads, and terminate(status, answer), with which the agent self-signals success or failure and optionally returns textual answer. We rely on terminate rather than an external success classifier: each rollout therefore carries its own coarse-grained label, which downstream filtering can use as soft quality signal without requiring per-task evaluators. Context windowing matched to the SFT format. High-resolution screenshots make every additional vision frame expensive. We keep the three most recent screenshots in vision form and convert older steps into short textual summary block (Old steps: . . . ) prepended Step 𝑘: to the system message. Crucially, this exact windowing scheme is reproduced during SFT conversion, so the supervised model is trained on the same context layout it will encounter at inference timethere is no train/test mismatch in how prior steps are presented. . . . Response: Reasoning: 3.4. From Trajectories to SFT Samples naive single-sample-per-trajectory conversion wastes most of the supervision in long rollout. We instead apply step-prefix expansion. Let trajectory 𝜏 consist of goal 𝑔, an initial screenshot 𝑠0, and sequence , where 𝑜𝑡 = (think𝑡, action𝑡) is the models output at step 𝑡 and 𝑠𝑡 is the resulting of step pairs {(𝑜𝑡, 𝑠𝑡)}𝑇 𝑡=1 screenshot. We expand 𝜏 into the supervised set 𝒟(𝜏 ) = {(𝑥𝑡, 𝑜𝑡)}𝑇 𝑡=1, 𝑥𝑡 = (𝑔, 𝑉𝑡(𝜏 ), 𝐻𝑡(𝜏 )), (3) where 𝑉𝑡(𝜏 ) retains the at-most-three most recent screenshots from {𝑠0, . . . , 𝑠𝑡1} in vision form and 𝐻𝑡(𝜏 ) summarizes the older {𝑜𝑖}𝑖𝑡4 as textual block. Each prefix length therefore contributes its own training sample, exposing the SFT model to every state the actor saw during rolloutincluding very early states, where most teaching about how to begin task is concentratedrather than only the final state of completed runs. Each sample is emitted in the LLaMA-Factory ShareGPT format with images interleaved into the conversation via <image> placeholders and the corresponding paths listed in parallel images field. The supervision target is the chain of thought wrapped in <think>...</think>, followed by the ## Action: / ## Code: block; the user-facing turns reproduce the same system prompt, optional Old steps:. . . summary (active only when 𝑡 > 3), and instruction template used at rollout time. We discard any sample whose referenced screenshots are missing, then shuffle and shard the remaining records. 3.5. Scaling Synthetic CUA Collection Generating millions of steps requires running thousands of full Linux desktops in parallel, which is the practical bottleneck of CUA data collection rather than model inference. Two architectural decisions made this tractable on heterogeneous compute. Decoupled inference and environment. The VLM is served as stateless OpenAI-compatible vLLM endpoint (Ray-based, tensor-parallel across the models GPUs); each data-collector process owns its own VM and talks to the endpoint over HTTP. Because the actor is stateless and the environment is the slow side, we can scale model and environment fleets independently. Within single Slurm reservation we colocate the vLLM Ray cluster and the collectors on the same GPU nodes, using the otherwise-idle CPU cores on those nodes to host VMs; this avoids paying inter-node bandwidth for the dominant traffic of high-resolution screenshots. Pluggable VM backend. We implement two interchangeable VM backends behind common interface: Singularity (local). An OSWorld QEMU/KVM stack packaged as Singularity (Apptainer) .sif image that runs without root and without Docker daemon, which makes it deployable on standard HPC 6 ProCUA-SFT Technical Report Figure 2 (a) OSWorld success rate during SFT on UI-TARS 7B. ProCUA (blue) rises steadily from 11.7% to peak of 45.0% over 4,800 steps, while AgentNet (red) plateaus at 810% after one epoch (750 steps) below the pretrained UI-TARS-1.5-7B baseline of 26.3% (dashed). The light band shows the trend after moving-average smoothing. (b) Best checkpoint comparison: ProCUA reaches 45.0%, outperforming both the base model (26.3%) and AgentNet (10.0%) by 18.7 pp and 35.0 pp, respectively. (c) Ablation over data diversification strategies at matched training budget (700 steps). Round-robin sampling by app combination (By App Combo) achieves 30.9%, the only strategy to exceed the base-model baseline; selection by action set or the combined nested strategy underperforms the non-diversified variant (27.3%). nodes. When the host exposes /dev/kvm the VM uses hardware acceleration; otherwise the same image transparently falls back to software emulation, trading throughput for portability. NVCF (serverless). For pools where local KVM is unavailable or local capacity is saturated, the same collector code drives NVIDIA Cloud Functions (NVCF; NVIDIA Corporation, 2024), NVIDIAs serverless platform for GPU-attached workloads. NVCF handles auto-deploy, auto-scale, and auto-undeploy of the OSWorld container; we expose it to the rest of the pipeline through small local HTTPS-to-HTTP proxy that forwards the OSWorld API, Chrome DevTools, and VLC endpoints. To avoid burning paid GPU time on deploy that will fail anyway, we pre-download all setup files to local cache before committing the NVCF deploy, and only proceed if every download succeeds. Throughput-oriented orchestration. Each collector runs an asyncio thread pool with two stagesinit (sample config, boot the VM, apply the setup) and collect (synthesize goal, roll out actions)so that slow boot never blocks an in-progress rollout. semaphore caps concurrent VMs per node; sequential boot delays prevent the thundering-herd that otherwise destabilizes the QEMU/KVM stack when many guests start at once. Trajectories are checkpointed to disk after every step so that node-level failure costs at most one in-flight rollout. [total trajectories / num resources (how many VMs used in parallel)] 4. Empirical Validation We continue training UI-Tars 1.5 7B on the Agent Net and ProCUA datasets for 1 epoch. We pack samples to maximum sequence length of 32k, with batch size of 512, learning rate 2𝑒 5, using cosine learning rate scheduler, and weight decay of 0.1. After 1 epoch of training, ProCUA reaches 44.99% success rate on OSWorld, 18.68% improvement over the original UI-Tars performance. On the other hand, AgentNet only leads to degradation from the original model, plateauing at 8-10%. Notably, at similar number of training steps, the ProCUA dataset is able to recover the performance of the original UI-Tars performance. Comparing ProCUA to AgentNet, we can identify several factors that likely contribute to ProCUAs stronger OSWorld performance over AgentNet. First, ProCua trajectories are markedly longer-horizon: their mean length is roughly 1.8X that of AgentNets (30 vs 17 steps) and their tail extends much farther, as shown in Figure 3 (left). Second, ProCUAs app coverage is more aligned with OSWorlds evaluation distribution. 7 ProCUA-SFT Technical Report Figure 3 Comparison of dataset statistics for ProCUA and AgentNet. Left: Distribution of trajectory lengths (steps per trajectory), with the 𝑦-axis on log scale. ProCUA trajectories are markedly longer-horizon (𝜇 29.7 vs. 18.6 steps) with substantially heavier tail. Center: Domain distribution across the twelve most frequent applications, as percentage of labeled trajectories. Right: Action category distribution, as percentage of all action calls; ProCUA relies more heavily on keyboard actions, whereas AgentNet is more click-dominated. The middle panel of Figure 3 shows that nearly half of all labeled ProCUA trajectories target LibreOffice applications (Impress 24%, Calc 17%, Writer 6%), and another 20% involve multi-application workflowsboth categories that dominate OSWorld. Note that the entire Windows/Mac portion of AgentNet (18k of 22.6k trajectories) omits app-level metadata in the released data, so this comparison is restricted to AgentNets 5k labeled (Ubuntu) trajectories. Third, the action mix differs in way that favors more deterministic interaction. As shown in Figure 3 (right), AgentNet trajectories are heavily click-dominated (63% of all calls vs. 41% for ProCUA), whereas ProCUA shifts much larger fraction of its actions onto keyboard primitives and text entry. Keyboard shortcuts and direct text input are inherently less brittle than pixel-accurate clicks, especially across the kind of fine-grained menu navigation and form-filling that OSWorld tasks demand. Diversification ablation. Since CUA training data is distributed unevenly across application combinations and action types, we ask: which axis of diversity matters most for downstream performance, and what is the best strategy to select diverse subset from large trajectory pool? We investigate this by fixing the training budget at 700 steps and comparing four data-selection strategies applied to the same 111,862-trajectory pool (Figure 2c). Three strategies use round-robin samplingby action set, by app combination, and nested combined strategy that round-robins over app-combination buckets and within each bucket over action-set sub-buckets, and no diversity-aware selection. trajectories. Round-robin by app combination achieves 30.9%, outperforming both the base-model baseline (26.3%) and the non-diversified variant (27.3%) by 4.6 pp and 3.6 pp, respectively. In contrast, selection by action set (24.9%) and the nested combined strategy (25.5%) both underperform the non-diversified baseline. This result identifies application-combination coverage as the dominant diversity axis for CUA SFT: ensuring broad representation across app combinations matters more than balancing action types. 5. Analysis 5.1. Trajectory Graph Analysis To characterize the structural complexity of GUI-agent trajectories, we model each trajectory as directed screen-transition graph: nodes represent distinct screens (identified by application and visual state), and edges represent transitions triggered by agent actions. From this graph we extract eight metrics that capture complementary aspects of navigational behavior: the number of screens (nodes) and transitions (edges); the number of cycles (circular navigation patterns indicating backtracking or retries); total actions performed; the count of distinct applications used and the number of app switches; linearity score (fraction of nodes with simple in/out flow; 1.0 denotes perfectly sequential chain); and screen-revisit ratio (fraction of screen visits that return to previously visited screen). 8 ProCUA-SFT Technical Report Figure 4 Six representative GUI-agent trajectories illustrating the spectrum of structural complexity in our dataset. Each panel shows the screen-transition graph (nodes colored by application), key graph metrics, the task goal, and trajectory summary with application-related terms color-coded to match their graph nodes. (a) purely linear, single-app workflow in GIMP (linearity = 1.00, 0 cycles). (b) twoapp information-gathering task across Chrome and LibreOffice Calc with moderate backtracking (4 cycles, linearity = 0.57). (c) four-app task with near-linear flow despite involving Writer, GIMP, Desktop, and Terminal (linearity = 0.80, 1 cycle). (d) file-conversion task traversing Calc, VS Code, Desktop, and Terminal with limited branching (linearity = 0.83, 5 app switches). (e) An email-composition task in Thunderbird exhibiting cyclic navigation due to error recovery (4 cycles, linearity = 0.71). (f) highly non-linear, multi-app data-extraction workflow across File Manager, Document Viewer, Desktop, and Calc, with the highest cycle count (10), lowest linearity (0.33), and most app switches (9) among the six examples. Dataset-level statistics. We profiled 34 854 trajectories spanning 61 unique application combinations. Across sample of 5000 profiled trajectories, the median trajectory visits 5 screens, traverses 5 transitions, and executes 19 actions. The median linearity score is 0.86, with 42.3% of trajectories being perfectly linear (linearity = 1.0) and only 2.2% exhibiting highly non-linear structure (linearity < 0.5). Approximately 35.8% of trajectories are acyclic, 56.7% contain 15 cycles, and 7.5% exhibit more than 5 cycles. Single-application trajectories account for 73.9% of the dataset (25,767 out of 34,854), while 18.5% involve two applications, 6.6% involve three, and 0.9% involve four or more. 9 ProCUA-SFT Technical Report Figure 5 Comparison of the full ProCUA training set (16 480 trajectories) and the ProCUA + RareApp Synthesis complexity subset (353 trajectories). Left: Overlaid histograms (percentage-normalized) for six topology metrics; dashed lines mark per-dataset means. Right: App-combination distribution (top 15 by ProCUA + Rare-App Synthesis frequency). Multi-app combinations are shown in bold. The rare-app subset shifts toward more applications per trajectory (mean 3.3 vs. 2.0), more app switches (4.8 vs. 2.0), more screens (8.6 vs. 6.4), and more total actions (35.2 vs. 26.6), while maintaining comparable cycle counts and only moderately lower linearity (0.80 vs. 0.84). The right panel shows that single-app categories still appear in ProCUA + Rare-App Synthesis but at reduced frequency, while multi-app combinations (e.g., Chrome + LO Calc, Files + LO Writer, Desktop + VS Code) emerge as substantial fraction of the subset. Key findings from representative examples. Figure 4 presents six trajectories selected to illustrate the full complexity spectrum. Three patterns emerge: 1. Linear single-app workflows (panel a) proceed through chain of distinct screens without revisiting any state. Despite visiting 7 screens and executing 35 actions, the GIMP image-editing task achieves perfect linearity (1.00) with zero cycles, indicating that the agent completes each sub-task (crop, resize, export) in single forward pass. 2. Multi-app tasks with moderate branching (panels be) introduce cycles when the agent must switch contexts or recover from errors. In panel (b), the agent backtracks between paper detail view and its PDF version in Chrome before pasting results into Calc (6 backtracks, 4 cycles). Panel (e) shows error-recovery branching: the Thunderbird draft-save shortcut triggers an error dialog, forcing the agent to dismiss and retry (4 cycles, revisit ratio = 0.84). Panels (c) and (d) involve four applications each but maintain relatively linear structure (linearity 0.80) because the agent moves through apps in largely sequential order. 3. Highly non-linear multi-app workflows (panel f) are characterized by dense graphs with many cycles. The invoice-extraction task requires repeatedly navigating between the file manager, document viewer, and spreadsheet, producing 10 cycles, 13 backtracks, the lowest linearity (0.33), highest graph density (0.37), and 9 app switches. Its transition efficiency of 0.17 (unique screens per action) is the lowest among the six examples, reflecting the high cost of inter-application coordination. These examples demonstrate that trajectory complexity is not simply function of the number of applications involved: panels (c) and (d) involve four apps yet remain largely linear, while panel (f) also involves four apps but produces highly cyclic graph. The critical factor is the pattern of inter-app coordinationtasks requiring repeated cross-referencing between applications (e.g., extracting data from multiple PDFs into spreadsheet) yield fundamentally different graph topologies than tasks where applications are visited sequentially. 10 ProCUA-SFT Technical Report 5.2. Complexity-Aware Data Curation key challenge in training GUI agents is the long-tailed distribution of application combinations: common single-app tasks (e.g., editing spreadsheet) dominate the training corpus, while complex multi-app workflows that require inter-application coordination are rare. We address this through two-stage pipeline: (1) identifying rare, structurally complex trajectories and using them to synthesize new training data, and (2) selecting diverse subsets via round-robin sampling. Rare-app identification and synthesis. Using the app-combination labels extracted during profiling (5.1), we identify 2,065 trajectories whose app combinations occur 3 times across the 111 862-trajectory poolthe long tail of rare multi-app workflows. We then use the goals and trajectory summaries of these rare examples as few-shot demonstrations in our task synthesis pipeline to generate new training tasks that target underrepresented multi-app coordination patterns. The resulting ProCUA + Rare-App Synthesis subset (353 trajectories) is collected by executing the synthesized tasks and profiled with full topology metrics. Topology metric shifts. Figure 5 compares the full ProCUA set and the rare-app subset across six topology metrics. The subset exhibits consistently higher structural complexity: the mean number of unique applications increases from 2.0 to 3.3 (median: 2 3), and mean app switches more than double from 2.0 to 4.8 (median: 0 4). Trajectories visit more screens (8.6 vs. 6.4) and execute more GUI actions (35.2 vs. 26.6, median: 29 vs. 18). The mean linearity score decreases only modestly from 0.84 to 0.80, indicating that even complex multi-app workflows often follow largely sequential pattern. Cycle counts are comparable (2.8 vs. 2.6), suggesting that navigation loops arise from task structure (error recovery, data verification) rather than from application count. App-combination distribution. The full training set is dominated by single-app trajectories: LO Impress (13.3%), LO Calc (10.5%), and Chrome (9.7%) together account for over one-third of all data. In the rare-app subset, these categories appear at reduced frequency (8.2%, 6.2%, 3.1%), while multi-app combinations emerge: Chrome + LO Calc, Files + LO Writer, Desktop + VS Code, and three-app workflows such as Chrome + Files + Terminal each represent 0.62.0% of the subset but <0.1% of the full set. 6. Conclusion We introduced ProCUA-SFT, an open dataset of 3.1M SFT samples from 93K fully synthetic computer-use trajectories. On OSWorld, ProCUA-SFT outperforms human demonstrations for computer-use SFT. One epoch on UI-TARS 7B reaches 45.0%+18.7 pp over the base model and +35 pp over AgentNets 22.5K human trajectories. Three design choices prove critical: grounded task synthesis with in-loop precondition verification ensures that generated goals are feasible in the current desktop state; seeding desktops with real-world spreadsheets and presentations produces tasks requiring genuine cross-application reasoning; and using single VLM for goal generation, verification, and execution closes the planneractor capability gap. We plan to iterate on ProCUA-SFT as stronger open-weight VLMs, additional OS platforms, and external reward models become available.
